title: About
text: HI! I’m Gavyn, a student at the University of Nebraska-Lincoln. I love the sports data that most don’t talk about and I love to share it!

title: Who is Punter University?
Does Pace of Play affect Winning in the NBA?
text:
As a basketball connoisseur myself, and also a slow runner, I love to slow the pace of the game down and take things easy, as they come to me. So I’ve wondered, does pace of play affect winning?
Pace of play is the average amount of possessions that a certain team has per game. If a team has 104 possessions one game, and 60 the next, their pace of play would be 82 possessions per game. This number calculated is that teams average pace.
But the real question is : Does pace of play affect winning? Looking at play by play data from the NBA since 2005 in the line chart below, we can see that the average pace of play over the years has been changing constantly. It was very high in the early 2000’s and then dipped low and remained that way until the lockout season in 2010-11. It then skyrocketed and has remained high since.


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(ggrepel)

averages_per_year <- read_csv("posts/Pace of Play/pace_data/nba_averages_80-25.csv")

wins <- read_csv("posts/Pace of Play/pace_data/wins_per_year.csv")

all_teams <- read_csv("posts/Pace of Play/pace_data/pace_and_win.csv")

averages_per_year <- averages_per_year |>
  select(Season, Pace)

win_pct <- wins |>
  pivot_longer(-Season, names_to = "team", values_to = "wins") |>
  mutate(
    win_pct = wins / 82
  )
ggplot(averages_per_year, aes(x = Season, y = Pace, group = 1)) +
  geom_line(color = "black") +
  geom_point(color = "grey40") +
  geom_text_repel(
    data = subset(averages_per_year,
                  Season %in% c("2011-12", "2019-20")),
    aes(label = ifelse(Season == "2011-12", "Lockout", "COVID"))
  ) +
  scale_x_discrete(
    breaks = averages_per_year$Season[seq(1, nrow(averages_per_year), by = 5)]
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "NBA Pace of Play Has Changed Over Time",
    subtitle = "Average possessions per game across all NBA teams (1980–2025)",
    x = "Season",
    y = "Pace",
    caption = "Source: NBA Data | By Gavyn Klommhaus"
  ) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 18, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 12),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 10),
    plot.caption = element_text(size = 10)
  )









Now the question to answer : does this affect winning? Looking at data that compares the winning percentage and the average pace of play per season for each NBA team since 2005, we can see that there is no correlation between the pace of play of a team and their winning percentage.
As we can see in the scatterplot below, there appears to be no correlation between the average pace of play and winning percentage. Highlighted are some of the most recognized “best teams” since 2005 and it shows that even comparing those teams, their winning percentage was all high, but their pace of play was all over the spectrum.


Code
ggplot() + 
  geom_point(
    data = all_teams,
    aes(x = pace, y = win_pct),
    color = "grey30",
    alpha = .3
  ) + 
  geom_point(
    data = subset(all_teams,
      (team == "GSW" & Season == "2015-16") |
      (team == "MIA" & Season == "2012-13") |
      (team == "LAL" & Season == "2009-10") |
      (team == "SAS" & Season == "2013-14") |
      (team == "BOS" & Season == "2023-24") |
      (team == "DAL" & Season == "2010-11")
    ),
    aes(x = pace, y = win_pct),
    color = "red",
    size = 2
  ) + 
  geom_smooth(
    data = all_teams,
    aes(x = pace, y = win_pct),
    method = "lm",
    se = FALSE,
    color = "black"
  ) +
  geom_text_repel(
    data = subset(all_teams,
      (team == "GSW" & Season == "2015-16") |
      (team == "MIA" & Season == "2012-13") |
      (team == "LAL" & Season == "2009-10") |
      (team == "SAS" & Season == "2013-14") |
      (team == "BOS" & Season == "2023-24") |
      (team == "DAL" & Season == "2010-11")
    ),
    aes(x = pace, y = win_pct, label = paste(team, Season))
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Pace",
    y = "Win Percentage",
    title = "Winning Does Not Rely on Playing Fast",
    subtitle = "Highlighting Great Teams of the last 2 Decades (2005-2026)",
    caption = "Source: NBA Play-by-Play Data | By Gavyn Klommhaus"
  ) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 18, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 12),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 10),
    plot.caption = element_text(size = 10)
  )









To really put this into perspective, we grouped the pace of the teams in 6 categories : slowest, slower, slow, fast, faster, fastest. Putting each team from their respective year into each category, we can see still, that each bar is roughly the same showing that there is no correlation between pace of play and winning basketball games.


Code
all_teams <- all_teams |>
  mutate(pace_group = case_when(
    pace < 90 ~ "Slowest",
    pace < 92 ~ "Slower",
    pace < 94 ~ "Slow",
    pace < 96 ~ "Fast",
    pace < 98 ~ "Faster",
    TRUE ~ "Fastest"
  ))

all_teams |>
  group_by(pace_group) |>
  summarise(avg_win = mean(win_pct)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = pace_group, y = avg_win)) +
  geom_col(color = "black", fill = "grey60") +
  labs(
    title = "Average Win Percentage by Pace Group",
    subtitle = "Comparing team success across different pace tiers (2005–2026)",
    x = "Pace Group",
    y = "Average Win Percentage",
    caption = "Source: NBA Data | By Gavyn Klommhaus"
  ) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 18, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 12),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 10),
    plot.caption = element_text(size = 10)
  )









In conclusion, after discussing and grabbing and graphing data, we can see that there is absoulately no correaltion between a winning NBA team and how fast they play the game.
